items.title: US corporations have never had more foreign exposure: The proportion of S&P 500 revenue generated abroad is up to a record ~42%. This surpasses the previous peak of ~40% in 2015 and ~39% posted during the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble. This comes despite the unprecedented surge in domestic AI investment, which was expected to make corporate revenue more concentrated in the US. Technology companies are behind most of the surge, with foreign markets now accounting for over 50% of the sector's revenue. Furthermore, the communication services sector generates ~47% of its revenue abroad, up from less than 40% before the 2020 pandemic. The global economy is more connected than ever.
items.content:
US corporations have never had more foreign exposure:

The proportion of S&P 500 revenue generated abroad is up to a record ~42%.

This surpasses the previous peak of ~40% in 2015 and ~39% posted during the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble.

This comes despite the unprecedented surge in domestic AI investment, which was expected to make corporate revenue more concentrated in the US.

Technology companies are behind most of the surge, with foreign markets now accounting for over 50% of the sector's revenue.

Furthermore, the communication services sector generates ~47% of its revenue abroad, up from less than 40% before the 2020 pandemic.

The global economy is more connected than ever.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: The South Korean and US AI trades are becoming increasingly synchronized: The 60-day correlation between the KOSPI and Nasdaq 100 index is up to +0.46, the highest since July 2024. This marks a sharp reversal from a negative correlation of -0.20 recorded in March. By comparison, the 5-year average is just +0.16 or nearly one-third the 60-day metric. Furthermore, the Nasdaq 100's sensitivity to declines in South Korean stocks is up to its highest level since 1990. At the same time, the MSCI World Index's sensitivity to the South Korean market is up to its highest in 4 years. In other words, declines in South Korean equities are increasingly serving as an early read on US and global equity ris
items.content:
The South Korean and US AI trades are becoming increasingly synchronized:

The 60-day correlation between the KOSPI and Nasdaq 100 index is up to +0.46, the highest since July 2024.

This marks a sharp reversal from a negative correlation of -0.20 recorded in March.

By comparison, the 5-year average is just +0.16 or nearly one-third the 60-day metric.

Furthermore, the Nasdaq 100's sensitivity to declines in South Korean stocks is up to its highest level since 1990.

At the same time, the MSCI World Index's sensitivity to the South Korean market is up to its highest in 4 years.

In other words, declines in South Korean equities are increasingly serving as an early read on US and global equity risk appetite, particularly in technology stocks.

The AI trade has never been more global.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Stress in one of the credit market's riskiest areas is intensifying: Collateralized loan obligation (CLO) equity tranches returned -15% in Q1 2026, their worst quarterly performance since the 2020 pandemic crash. These tranches represent the highest-risk layer of the $1.3 trillion CLO market, with equity investors taking the first losses when underlying loans underperform. This also marks the 2nd consecutive quarterly loss and even exceeds the Q2 2022 bear market drop of -12%. The selloff has been driven by a decline in software loan prices combined with fewer new corporate loans being issued. This leaves CLO managers, the firms responsible for selecting the loans inside CLO portfolios, with
items.content:
Stress in one of the credit market's riskiest areas is intensifying:

Collateralized loan obligation (CLO) equity tranches returned -15% in Q1 2026, their worst quarterly performance since the 2020 pandemic crash.

These tranches represent the highest-risk layer of the $1.3 trillion CLO market, with equity investors taking the first losses when underlying loans underperform.

This also marks the 2nd consecutive quarterly loss and even exceeds the Q2 2022 bear market drop of -12%.

The selloff has been driven by a decline in software loan prices combined with fewer new corporate loans being issued.

This leaves CLO managers, the firms responsible for selecting the loans inside CLO portfolios, with fewer opportunities to invest in attractive assets, putting further pressure on returns.

Credit market stress is rising beneath the surface.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: BREAKING: President Trump is imposing an additional 50% tariff on a range of Canadian goods in response to "trade discrimination." Details include: 1. President Trump signed 3 proclamations today targeting different sets of Canadian imports 2. The tariffs are targeting separate areas where the US says it has experienced discrimination 3. The tariffs fall under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930 These tariffs are set to take effect in 30 days.
items.content:
BREAKING: President Trump is imposing an additional 50% tariff on a range of Canadian goods in response to "trade discrimination."

Details include:

1. President Trump signed 3 proclamations today targeting different sets of Canadian imports

2. The tariffs are targeting separate areas where the US says it has experienced discrimination 

3. The tariffs fall under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930

These tariffs are set to take effect in 30 days.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: The AI boom is creating a generational divide in software jobs: US software developer employment for those aged 22-25 has declined -23% since November 2022, when ChatGPT was launched. At the same time, the 26-30 age group has seen a decrease of -5%. By comparison, employment among software developers aged 41-49 has risen +18% over the same period. This creates a 41 percentage point gap between the youngest and one of the most experienced groups within the same profession. Meanwhile, early-career workers in the most AI-exposed jobs have experienced a -16% decline in employment relative to less-exposed occupations and more experienced peers since November 2022, even after adjusting for firm-sp
items.content:
The AI boom is creating a generational divide in software jobs:

US software developer employment for those aged 22-25 has declined -23% since November 2022, when ChatGPT was launched.

At the same time, the 26-30 age group has seen a decrease of -5%.

By comparison, employment among software developers aged 41-49 has risen +18% over the same period.

This creates a 41 percentage point gap between the youngest and one of the most experienced groups within the same profession.

Meanwhile, early-career workers in the most AI-exposed jobs have experienced a -16% decline in employment relative to less-exposed occupations and more experienced peers since November 2022, even after adjusting for firm-specific shocks.

By contrast, employment among workers in less AI-exposed fields, as well as more experienced workers within the same occupations, has remained steady or continued to grow.

The AI revolution is wiping out entry-level tech jobs.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: Leverage is rapidly unwinding across Chinese stocks: Margin debt on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges fell -2.8% on Friday, or -$11.7 billion, to $405 billion, the largest daily decline since January 2016. This also marks the 4th consecutive daily decrease, totaling -$36.9 billion. This comes as the Star 50 Index, which tracks Chinese technology stocks, plunged -7.1% on Friday, its 2nd-largest daily drop this year, while the CSI 300 fell -3.6%. Memory chip stocks were at the center of the selloff after attracting the highest levels of margin borrowing. As a result, their sharp selloff triggered margin calls, forcing investors to sell and accelerating the broader market decline. Chip stocks
items.content:
Leverage is rapidly unwinding across Chinese stocks:

Margin debt on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges fell -2.8% on Friday, or -$11.7 billion, to $405 billion, the largest daily decline since January 2016.

This also marks the 4th consecutive daily decrease, totaling -$36.9 billion.

This comes as the Star 50 Index, which tracks Chinese technology stocks, plunged -7.1% on Friday, its 2nd-largest daily drop this year, while the CSI 300 fell -3.6%.

Memory chip stocks were at the center of the selloff after attracting the highest levels of margin borrowing.

As a result, their sharp selloff triggered margin calls, forcing investors to sell and accelerating the broader market decline.

Chip stocks have become a global amplifier of market volatility.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: BREAKING: Mediators are working to push the US and Iran into a new ceasefire, per WSJ. Details include: 1. Mediators are worried the US and Iran could settle into a pattern where they keep conflict below the level of all-out war but disrupt regional economies indefinitely 2. Traffic in the Strait of Hormuz has now collapsed to just 10 ships per day 3. Qatar has floated the idea of a 10-day ceasefire and an end to restrictions on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz to cool tensions Brent crude oil prices continue to trade around $90/barrel.
items.content:
BREAKING: Mediators are working to push the US and Iran into a new ceasefire, per WSJ.

Details include:

1. Mediators are worried the US and Iran could settle into a pattern where they keep conflict below the level of all-out war but disrupt regional economies indefinitely

Brent crude oil prices continue to trade around $90/barrel.

items.title: US stocks have almost never moved this independently of each other: The S&P 500's 6-month implied correlation is down to 0.15, an all-time low. Implied correlation measures how closely stocks are expected to move together, with a reading this low meaning individual stocks are becoming increasingly disconnected from the broader index. This metric has now more than HALVED over the last 4 months, when correlation was at 0.35 during the market selloff. By comparison, the long-term average stands at 0.43, nearly 3 times the current level, while the high over this period was 0.82 during the 2020 pandemic crash. This historic divergence highlights a market increasingly driven by a handful of mega-c
items.content:
US stocks have almost never moved this independently of each other:

The S&P 500's 6-month implied correlation is down to 0.15, an all-time low.

Implied correlation measures how closely stocks are expected to move together, with a reading this low meaning individual stocks are becoming increasingly disconnected from the broader index.

This metric has now more than HALVED over the last 4 months, when correlation was at 0.35 during the market selloff.

By comparison, the long-term average stands at 0.43, nearly 3 times the current level, while the high over this period was 0.82 during the 2020 pandemic crash.

This historic divergence highlights a market increasingly driven by a handful of mega-cap stocks, while the rest of the market trades more on its own fundamentals.

Market concentration has rarely been this extreme.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: Hedge funds are dumping US tech stocks at a record pace: Hedge funds have sold information technology stocks in 6 of the last 8 weeks. This brings total 8-week sales to the largest in at least 10 years. Last week alone, technology was the most-sold US sector among hedge funds. As a result, tech exposure as a % of total market exposure is down to its lowest since February 2026. At this rate, tech exposure could fall to its lowest in at least 5 years as early as next week. Hedge funds are rapidly moving to the sidelines.
items.content:
Hedge funds are dumping US tech stocks at a record pace:

Hedge funds have sold information technology stocks in 6 of the last 8 weeks.

This brings total 8-week sales to the largest in at least 10 years.

Last week alone, technology was the most-sold US sector among hedge funds.

As a result, tech exposure as a % of total market exposure is down to its lowest since February 2026.

At this rate, tech exposure could fall to its lowest in at least 5 years as early as next week.

Hedge funds are rapidly moving to the sidelines.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: BREAKING: Alphabet, $GOOGL, is planning on launching a new “frozen” chip to run its AI models more efficiently, per The Information. Details include: 1. This new server chip would directly integrate the blueprint of its Gemini AI model 2. The chip would then enable Alphabet to provide its AI models to users much more efficiently 3. The chip's developers estimate that it would be 6 to 10 times more efficient than the most recent version of Google's existing line of AI chips Another tech giant has entered the chip race.
items.content:
BREAKING: Alphabet,  $GOOGL , is planning on launching a new “frozen” chip to run its AI models more efficiently, per The Information.

Details include:

1. This new server chip would directly integrate the blueprint of its Gemini AI model

2. The chip would then enable Alphabet to provide its AI models to users much more efficiently 

3. The chip's developers estimate that it would be 6 to 10 times more efficient than the most recent version of Google's existing line of AI chips

Another tech giant has entered the chip race.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: AI is accelerating US business formation: The number of businesses in AI-related sectors, including professional, scientific, and technical services, has grown +45% since the launch of ChatGPT in November 2022. By comparison, the number of construction businesses has increased +10% over the same period. Furthermore, business creation has risen +20% across the broader US economy. The Census Bureau now projects +29,700 new businesses will form monthly nationwide over the next year, up +17% YoY. Professional services are expected to account for more than 5,000 new businesses per month, a record for the sector and up +24% YoY. AI is fueling a new wave of entrepreneurship.
items.content:
AI is accelerating US business formation:

The number of businesses in AI-related sectors, including professional, scientific, and technical services, has grown +45% since the launch of ChatGPT in November 2022.

By comparison, the number of construction businesses has increased +10% over the same period.

Furthermore, business creation has risen +20% across the broader US economy.

The Census Bureau now projects +29,700 new businesses will form monthly nationwide over the next year, up +17% YoY.

Professional services are expected to account for more than 5,000 new businesses per month, a record for the sector and up +24% YoY.

AI is fueling a new wave of entrepreneurship.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: Everyone wants US stocks. Foreign private sector net purchases of US stocks rose +$35.2 billion MoM in May, to $121 billion, the 2nd-largest inflow on record. This marks the 2nd consecutive monthly increase. By comparison, the record monthly inflow was ~$130 billion in November 2024. Year-to-date, foreign private investors have purchased ~$270 billion of US stocks. To put this into perspective, foreign investors dumped -$31 billion and -$28 billion of South Korean stocks in June and May, respectively, the two largest monthly outflows on record. Furthermore, foreign investors also sold -$18 billion of Taiwanese stocks in June, the 2nd-largest sale on record. Global capital continues to favor
items.content:
Everyone wants US stocks.

Foreign private sector net purchases of US stocks rose +$35.2 billion MoM in May, to $121 billion, the 2nd-largest inflow on record.

This marks the 2nd consecutive monthly increase.

By comparison, the record monthly inflow was ~$130 billion in November 2024.

Year-to-date, foreign private investors have purchased ~$270 billion of US stocks.

To put this into perspective, foreign investors dumped -$31 billion and -$28 billion of South Korean stocks in June and May, respectively, the two largest monthly outflows on record.

Furthermore, foreign investors also sold -$18 billion of Taiwanese stocks in June, the 2nd-largest sale on record. 

Global capital continues to favor US equities over the rest of the world.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 19, 2026
